title: Sony files new patent for AI ghost gaming technology • Межа
text:
Mezha
News
Sony patents 'AI ghost' technology to help players through difficult gameplay moments
Vlad Cherevko
I have been interested in various types of electronics and technology since the early 2000s. I love playing computer games and understand how different gadgets work. I regularly follow news from the world of technology and write articles about it myself.
5 January, 02:56
0
Sony has filed a new patent that describes the technology of "AI ghosts" - digital assistants capable of supporting players in real time during the passage of difficult episodes in video games. This
is reported
by NotebookCheck with
reference
to Boing Boing. According to the document, these NPC assistants will be able to take control of the character, track the user's actions and offer personalized tips.
Unlike traditional static hints in tutorial sections, AI Ghosts will be created based on models trained on thousands of hours of gameplay footage from streaming platforms, social media, and YouTube, and will be able to assist players in solving puzzles or battling powerful and challenging enemies.
There are several modes of use: from simple hints with a demonstration of button presses to full control over the player's actions in difficult situations. The system will be able to additionally collect data from servers, track eye movements or use a Sony-licensed camera for more accurate contextual analysis.
The development is positioned as an extension of the Game Help feature on PlayStation 5 and could be useful for beginners or people with disabilities. At the same time, critics have expressed concerns about the technology's possible intrusiveness and reducing the value of independently overcoming game challenges.
Last month, Sony also filed a patent for a real-time content censorship system that could blur scenes of violence, hide profanity, or change dialogue based on players' ages and parental control settings. Some in the community saw this as an interference with the developers' original intent.
It is currently unknown whether these patents will be implemented in commercial products. They may remain only research developments, but their appearance has sparked a discussion about the balance between innovation, privacy, and authenticity of the gameplay.
